message:
feat: cut 6 weak exhibits, add 6 compelling new ones

Cut (simulated/redundant):
- Exhibit 2 (Runtime Creation) — redundant with #10
- Exhibit 4 (Task Lifecycle) — redundant with #12
- Exhibit 7 (Scheduler Lanes) — simulated, not real WASM
- Exhibit 9 (Two-Phase walkthrough) — simulated, not real WASM
- Exhibit 15 (Live Fetch) — redundant with #16
- Exhibit 21 (ABI Compat) — not viscerally compelling

New (each "impossible elsewhere" with rich narrative):

Exhibit 22: Deterministic Replay — runs same 15-op sequence
twice, proves byte-for-byte identical slot assignments. Makes
Heisenbug reproduction possible.

Exhibit 23: Handle Leak Detection — spawns 5 tasks, intentionally
forgets to join them, closes scope, proves cascade catches all
orphans. In tokio these become zombies.

Exhibit 24: Scope-as-Timeout — 3 tasks with 2s scope deadline,
live countdown, auto-cancel on deadline. One line vs 11
coordination points in vanilla JS.

Exhibit 25: Real WebSocket — opens wss://echo.websocket.events,
sends/receives through runtime, close-scope kills socket. No
zombie connections possible.

Exhibit 26: Code Comparison — side-by-side vanilla JS (22 lines,
11 bug-hiding coordination points) vs asupersync (6 lines, 1
coordination point) for same timeout+fetch scenario.

Exhibit 27: Rapid Scope Churn Benchmark — measures real WASM
scope create/close ops per second. Proves structural guarantees
don't sacrifice performance.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

Structured Concurrency — Region Tree

- -
-
-
- Exhibit 4 -

Task Lifecycle — Spawn, Join, Cancel

-

- Tasks are spawned within regions and produce outcomes when joined. Unlike tokio's fire-and-forget - spawn(), every task is owned — the region cannot close until - all its tasks complete. Cancellation is a structured protocol, not a silent drop. -

-
- -
-
-
-
- JavaScript - -
-
// Spawn a task in the current scope -const task = scope.spawnTask({ - label: 'compute-result' -}).value; - -// Join with a successful outcome -const result = task.join( - Outcome.ok(42) -); -// → { outcome: "ok", value: 42 } - -// Or cancel a running task -const task2 = scope.spawnTask({ - label: 'long-running' -}).value; -task2.cancel('user', 'User clicked stop'); -// → Cancelled outcome
-

Cancellation Protocol — Not a Silent Drop

- Exhibit 7 -

Three-Lane Priority Scheduler

-

- The scheduler dispatches tasks through three priority lanes: - Cancel (highest) → - Timed (EDF) → - Ready (default). - Cancel preemption is bounded — ready work always gets a dispatch slot within - cancel_streak_limit + 1 steps. -

Budget Algebra — Semiring Composition

- Exhibit 9 -

Two-Phase Effects — Reserve / Commit

-

- Anywhere cancellation could lose data, Asupersync uses a two-phase protocol: - reserve a slot (cancel-safe, nothing committed), - then commit (linear, cannot fail). - Dropping a reserved permit aborts cleanly — no half-sent messages, ever. -

-
- -
-
-
-

Clean. - -permit.send(message); // ← Phase 2: linear, cannot fail -// Message is now committed. No partial state. - -// Compare with tokio: -// tx.send(msg).await? ← if cancelled mid-send, -// message may be lost silently

Sibling Scope Isolation — No Cross-Contamination

+
+
+
+ Exhibit 22 — Impossible Elsewhere +

Deterministic Replay — Same Ops, Same Slots, Every Time

+

+ When debugging a concurrency bug in tokio or async-std, you're at the mercy of non-deterministic scheduling. + Run the same test twice and you get different behavior. Asupersync's handle allocator uses a + deterministic LIFO free-list — the same sequence of + create/close operations always produces identical slot assignments. This means you can + replay a failing execution exactly, down to every handle slot + and generation counter. This demo runs the same 15-operation sequence twice and proves byte-for-byte + identical allocation. +

+
+
+
+
+
Deterministic Allocation Proof
+ +
+
+
Runs the same 15 operations twice and compares every slot assignment
+
+
+
+
+
+ + +
+
+
+ Exhibit 23 — Impossible Elsewhere +

Handle Leak Detection — The Runtime Catches Your Mistakes

+

+ What happens when a programmer forgets to join a spawned task? In tokio, the task becomes an orphan — + it runs indefinitely with no owner, leaking memory and potentially holding locks. In Asupersync, + the scope won't let you. Tasks are pinned on spawn and + tracked as obligations. When the parent scope closes, it cascade-closes every unjoinable child. + The runtime cleans up your mistakes structurally. This demo intentionally "forgets" to join 5 tasks + and proves the scope catches all of them. +

+
+
+
+
+
Obligation Enforcement
+ +
+
+
Spawns 5 tasks and intentionally "forgets" to join them
+
+
+
+
Leak Detection
+
+
+
+
+
+ + +
+
+
+ Exhibit 24 — Impossible Elsewhere +

Scope-as-Timeout — Structural Deadlines, Not Afterthoughts

+

+ In vanilla JavaScript, implementing a timeout for a group of concurrent operations requires + creating an AbortController, a setTimeout, + wiring the signal to every fetch(), handling the + AbortError in every .catch(), + and remembering to clearTimeout on success. That's 6 coordination points + where bugs hide. In Asupersync, you create a scope and close it when the deadline fires. + Every task and fetch handle under it is automatically cancelled. One line of cleanup instead of six. +

+
+
+
+
+
Structural Timeout (2s deadline)
+
+ +
+
+
+
Spawns 3 tasks with a 2-second scope deadline — scope auto-closes when time's up
+
+
+
+
Timeout Events
+
+
+
+
+
+ + +
+
+
+ Exhibit 25 — Impossible Elsewhere +

WebSocket with Structured Cancellation

+

+ Browser WebSockets have no concept of "ownership." You call new WebSocket(url), + it exists in the global scope, and if your component unmounts you'd better remember to call ws.close() + — otherwise you have a zombie connection leaking messages into nothing. In Asupersync, the WebSocket handle is + scoped to a region. Close the region and the socket is automatically + closed with proper cleanup. The socket cannot outlive its scope. + This demo opens a real WebSocket, sends messages through the runtime, then closes the scope. +

+
+
+
+
+
Scoped WebSocket Lifecycle
+
+ + + +
+
+
+
Opens a real WebSocket scoped to a region — close the scope to auto-close the socket
+
+
+
+
WebSocket Events
+
+
+
+
+
+ + +
+
+
+ Exhibit 26 — The Whole Point +

Vanilla JS vs Asupersync — Side-by-Side

+

+ Every exhibit above demonstrates a structural guarantee. But the real question is: + how much code do YOU have to write? + Here's the same scenario — 3 concurrent fetches with a 5-second timeout — implemented + both ways. Count the coordination points where bugs can hide. +

+
+
+
+
+
+ Vanilla JavaScript — 22 lines +
+
const controller = new AbortController(); // 1. create controller +const signal = controller.signal; // 2. extract signal + +// 3. wire timeout to abort +const timer = setTimeout(() => { + controller.abort(); // 4. abort on timeout +}, 5000); + +try { + const results = await Promise.all([ + fetch(url1, { signal }), // 5. pass signal + fetch(url2, { signal }), // 6. pass signal + fetch(url3, { signal }), // 7. pass signal + ]); + clearTimeout(timer); // 8. clear on success + return results; +} catch (err) { + clearTimeout(timer); // 9. clear on error too! + if (err.name === 'AbortError') { + // 10. distinguish timeout from real error + throw new Error('Request timed out'); + } + throw err; // 11. re-throw others +}
+
+
+ 11 coordination points where bugs can hide. Forget #8 or #9? Timer leaks. Forget #5-7? Request ignores timeout. Mix up #10? Wrong error type propagates. +
+
+
+
+
+ Asupersync — 6 lines +
+
const scope = rt.enterScope('requests').value; + +// All requests are scoped — no signal wiring needed +scope.fetchRequest({ url: url1, method: 'GET' }); +scope.fetchRequest({ url: url2, method: 'GET' }); +scope.fetchRequest({ url: url3, method: 'GET' }); + +// After 5s, close the scope — ALL requests auto-cancelled +setTimeout(() => scope.close(), 5000);
+
+
+ 1 coordination point. scope.close() handles everything. No signal wiring. No manual cleanup. No error-path bugs. The scope IS the AbortController, the timeout, and the cleanup — all in one structural primitive. +
+
+
+
+
+ + -
+
- Exhibit 21 — Impossible Elsewhere -

ABI Compatibility Probing — Version Negotiation at the Boundary

+ Exhibit 27 — Performance +

Rapid Scope Churn — How Fast Is the Runtime?

- When you update a JavaScript package that talks to a WASM module, how do you know they're still compatible? - Most runtimes: you find out when something crashes in production. Asupersync has - built-in version negotiation. Every API call can include a consumer version, - and the runtime rejects incompatible calls before any state is mutated. This demo probes - the real ABI boundary with different version combinations. + All these structural guarantees sound expensive. Are they? This benchmark creates and closes scopes + as fast as possible for 1 second, measuring real WASM operations per second. + Each operation includes handle allocation, generation tracking, parent registration, and cleanup — + all the bookkeeping that makes structured concurrency work. The result might surprise you.

-
+
-
Version Compatibility Matrix
- +
WASM Runtime Throughput
+
-
-
Click Run to probe the ABI boundary with different version combinations
+
+
Measures real scope create/close operations per second
